# Budget Briefing for Maria (Concerned Elementary Parent)

**What you need to know about the FY27 school budget -- and what it means for your kids**

---

**If you read nothing else:** The district is cutting 42 teachers, closing Dyer Elementary, and hasn't published class sizes at any school under the new plan. The board hasn't voted yet. March 30 is the likely vote. The June 9 referendum is your direct say.

---

## Your School May Be Affected

The superintendent's proposed budget **closes Dyer Elementary School** and redistricts those students to the four remaining schools (Brown, Kaler, Small, Skillin). If your children attend Dyer, they will move to a different school in September 2026.

The administration originally recommended closing Skillin -- which scored highest on their evaluation matrix (46 out of 46 points, $19.6M in 5-year savings). Board Chair DeAngelis argued at the March 2 workshop that Skillin serves the most vulnerable immigrant families. The superintendent pivoted to Dyer, which scored 20/46 ($12.8M savings). The no-closure option (keeping all five schools, deeper across-the-board cuts) scored 23/46 but was never fully costed.

If your kids attend one of the remaining four schools, expect larger class sizes as Dyer students are absorbed. The exact impact by school has not been published yet.

## 42 Teachers Are Being Cut

The proposed budget eliminates 42 teaching positions (from 354 to 312), a 12% reduction. Another 16 ed tech and clerk positions would go. That's 58 fewer adults in school buildings.

**What this means in practice:** The district hasn't released school-by-school class size projections under the new staffing model. This is a critical gap. Ask for it at the March 16 open conversation or March 23 workshop -- the numbers should exist before any board vote.

Specialist teachers (art, music, PE) and support staff are not broken out separately in public documents. If these matter to you, ask specifically: *"How many specialist FTE are in the proposed budget vs. this year?"*

## Is Elementary Taking a Disproportionate Hit?

The Dyer closure is the most visible cut in the entire budget. But the budget also includes "Grades 5-12 efficiencies" saving ~$1M from adjusted teaching loads and reduced course sections at the middle and high school. The administration has not published a breakdown of the 42 teacher cuts by level -- elementary vs. middle vs. high school. Without that breakdown, you can't tell whether elementary is absorbing more pain than its share. Ask for it.

For comparison: the athletic complex debt service payment ($99K) is in the budget. Capital projects continue. If you feel like elementary classrooms are being cut to protect other priorities, the data to confirm or refute that isn't public yet. Push for it.

## How South Portland Compares

You want benchmarks. Here's what's available:

| District | Per-Pupil Cost |
|----------|---------------|
| **South Portland** | **$26,651** |
| Portland | $25,891 |
| Yarmouth | $25,451 |
| Gorham | $20,305 |

South Portland spends 31% more per student than Gorham -- but South Portland's student body is dramatically more complex (17% multilingual learners, 23% special education, 10% McKinney-Vento eligible). That complexity costs money. The question isn't whether the district spends more, but whether the spending is allocated to the right places.

## The Numbers Behind the Cuts

The district has an **$8.4M gap** between what it costs to keep things as they are and what it can raise. The fund balance (savings) is gone -- down from $5.9M three years ago to essentially zero. The school portion of your property tax would have gone up 19% just to maintain current operations.

The proposed budget limits the increase to about 3.3%, but only by making deep cuts. City Council guidance suggests they'll accept 5-6% on the school side. The gap between 3.33% and 6% could restore roughly 25-30 positions. Ask the board: *"What would a 5% budget look like in terms of elementary staffing?"*

## Share This With Your Group Chat

> The school board is cutting 42 teachers and closing Dyer Elementary to close an $8.4M budget gap. They haven't published which schools lose which teachers, or what class sizes will look like. The board hasn't voted yet -- March 30 is the likely vote. The March 23 workshop at Redbank (6 PM) is the first time detailed numbers go public. June 9 referendum is our direct vote.

## What You Can Do

- **March 16 (6 PM, SPHS):** Open conversation with district leadership -- your best chance for direct Q&A
- **March 23 (6 PM, Redbank):** Budget Workshop II -- line-item detail will be presented
- **March 30 (6 PM, SPHS):** Budget Workshop III -- the board may vote on a budget to send to council
- **June 9: Vote in the referendum.** Public comment matters, but this is the only time you get a direct vote on the budget. Parent turnout changes outcomes.

## Questions Worth Asking

1. What are the projected class sizes at each elementary school under this budget?
2. How many specialist teachers (art, music, PE, library) are retained vs. cut?
3. How many of the 42 teacher cuts are at the elementary level vs. middle/high school?
4. What is the transition plan for Dyer families -- transportation, class placement, timeline?
5. What would a 5% or 6% budget look like in terms of restored elementary positions?

---

*Based on all public budget documents, school board meetings, and city council proceedings through March 10, 2026.*
